text:
Your working directory is a checkout of the real downshift repository at the commit where the bug below reproduces. Find and fix the bug in the library source.

Bug: in the `useCombobox` hook, when the menu is open and the user clicks one of the items to select it, the text input momentarily loses focus (a focus flicker). The item is still selected, but focus should never leave the input during a click-to-select — after clicking an item the input must remain focused (and show the selected item's value). The underlying cause is that a native mousedown on the item blurs the input before the selection is handled. The correct behavior is that `getItemProps()` returns an `onMouseDown` handler for each item that suppresses this default blur (so focus stays on the input), and this handler must compose correctly with a consumer-supplied `onMouseDown`: the consumer's handler still runs, and if the consumer marks the event with `preventDownshiftDefault` then downshift's own blur-suppression is skipped. Fix the library source so clicking an item keeps the input focused. Fix the library source (under `src/`); do not just add workarounds in test files.

Environment notes: dependencies are already installed (Node, npm). You can scope the existing Jest suite to whatever file you're iterating on with `npx kcd-scripts test --no-watch <path-to-file>`. Fix the library source; do not just add workarounds in test files.

message: `--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.
`--dangerously-bypass-hook-trust` is enabled. Enabled hooks may run without review for this invocation.

text: I’ll trace `useCombobox` item prop construction and the existing event-composition conventions, then patch the source and run the focused Jest coverage plus relevant checks.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc 'pwd && rg -n "getItemProps|preventDownshiftDefault|onMouseDown|useCombobox" src test __tests__ 2>/dev/null | head -240 && rg --files | head -80'

text: The hook already refocuses the input during `onClick`, but the browser’s earlier native `mousedown` still causes the blur/focus flicker. I’m matching the repository’s `callAllEventHandlers` contract so the new handler runs after the consumer handler and honors both synthetic and native `preventDownshiftDefault` markers.

input.command: /bin/bash -lc "sed -n '1,80p' src/hooks/useCombobox/index.js && sed -n '1,180p' src/hooks/useSelect/__tests__/getItemProps.test.js && rg -n \"preventDefault\\(\\)|onMouseDown\" src/hooks src/__tests__ test | head -120"

/bin/bash -lc "rg -n \"itemHandleMouseDown|onMouseDown: callAllEventHandlers|preventDefault.*inputRef|inputRef.current.focus\" node_modules/downshift src 2>/dev/null | head -80; sed -n '120,225p' src/hooks/useCombobox/index.js; sed -n '130,175p' src/__tests__/downshift.get-item-props.js"

text: The class-based implementation in this same repository already defines the intended behavior: compose the consumer `onMouseDown` first, then call `event.preventDefault()`, and expose that handler even for disabled items. I’ll apply that pattern to the hook, preserving the hook’s existing item click and mouse-move behavior.

text: The source patch is in place, including ordering `...rest` before the composed handler so a consumer-supplied `onMouseDown` cannot overwrite Downshift’s handler. The focused test process is still running; I’m waiting for its result, then I’ll add or run coverage for focus retention and the opt-out marker.

The focused `getItemProps` suite passes all 19 tests. The implementation also satisfies the opt-out semantics through the shared composer: a consumer handler runs first, and setting `preventDownshiftDefault` prevents the internal `preventDefault()` call. I’m running the complete `useCombobox` test directory and the project lint check now.

Added composed `onMouseDown` behavior that prevents input blur while preserving consumer handlers and `preventDownshiftDefault` opt-out support.
